query: Reasoning complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: 7 Conflict Resolution Tips for Couples [Updated 2026] | National University
results.content:
To do so, open the discussion by showing consideration for your loved one’s feelings by saying, “I care about our relationship.” Or, “I know you don’t mean to upset me.” Next, describe the behavior you’d like your partner to change in clear detail and express the feelings you are experiencing, whether you’re angry, hurt, irritated, frustrated, or confused. Then ask for a specific change, such as, “I would rather you speak to me in a quiet tone.” Or, “I would like it if you’d wait until I finish my sentence before talking.” Finally, ask for an agreement at the end of the request: “Are you willing to agree to that?” Using “I” statements helps reduce blaming language, lower defensiveness, and promote more collaborate conflict resolution1.

##### 2. Don’t Blame Your Partner [...] ##### 2. Don’t Blame Your Partner

The reasons why you may be tempted to blame your partner for problems are endless — whether it’s something they said, did, or didn’t do. But nothing good will come of it because the person taking the blame may feel attacked, forcing them to respond to the blame instead of the problem. It’s better to respond with “I feel” statements that keep the focus on the issue at hand. An example is: “I feel angry when I’m alone, and you’re out with your friends.” Or, “I feel frustrated that the trash hasn’t been taken out yet.” The approach of stating how you feel can lead to better communication. It honors the feelings of you and your partner without putting anyone down. For helpful examples, visit Relationship Center’s blog on the topic.

Vocabulary.com

Reasoning is a logical, thoughtful way of thinking. When your teacher explains the reasoning behind his classroom rules, he makes it clear exactly why and how he came up with them. Voters often want to understand the reasoning behind certain laws, and toddlers almost always want to know the reasoning behind rules about bedtime and wearing warm clothes on cold days. You can also use reasoning as an adjective, to describe someone who can think logically. A reasoning adult can make decisions for herself. The word comes from reason, which is rooted in the Old French raisoner, "discuss or argue," and the Late Latin rationare, "to discourse."

## Vocabulary lists containing reasoning

Vocabulary.com

### Argumentative Writing [...] # reasoning

## noun

the act or process of a person who reasons.

the process of forming conclusions, judgments, or inferences from facts or premises.

the reasons, arguments, proofs, etc., resulting from this process.

## noun

the act or process of drawing conclusions from facts, evidence, etc

the arguments, proofs, etc, so adduced

## Other Word Forms

## Etymology

### Origin of reasoning

First recorded in 1325–75, reasoning is from the Middle English word resoninge. See reason, -ing 2

## Explanation

Vocabulary.com

results.title: Understanding and Handling Conflict — the Ugly, the Bad, and the Good
results.content:
No one is wrong, nothing is broken; I’m not trying to “fix” anything
   Much of the time this is kind of like therapy; I can be a sounding board and offer ideas and perspectives, but it is ultimately nothing I can resolve myself; I can only help others find their own resolution
   Show compassion and care; I care about people (otherwise I wouldn’t be in management), and this is the place and time to really lean into that
   Sometimes the resolution is that someone realizes they’d be happier elsewhere, and that’s not only OK, it’s a great realization for them! But it’s also really, really stressful for them, so if it comes to that, support them as best as possible in their next steps. [...] Emotions tend to be high, and falling back to rationality and logic can sometimes hurt more than it helps. Don’t ignore or set aside logic and rationality entirely, but bring it in judiciously and acknowledge its limits given the context. I’ve found that the most effective approach is to remind people that logic is one of the tools at their disposal rather than use logic directly on the situation myself — let the people in the conflict take the lead on using that tool once you put it in front of them.

## reasoning

(rizənɪŋ  IPA Pronunciation Guide )

Word forms: plural reasonings  

variable noun

Reasoning is the process by which you reach a conclusion after thinking about all the facts.

...the reasoning behind the decision.

Synonyms:  argument, proof, interpretation, hypothesis   More Synonyms of reasoning [...] Derived forms

results.title: Conflict Management: Difficult Conversations with Difficult People - PMC
results.content:
Another aspect of preparation is to recognize your emotional response and how it might affect your view of the situation. Addressing a difficult situation when one is angry or frustrated is more likely to be ineffective than when one is calm. Several famous quotes illustrate the point.

“Speak when you are angry and you will make the best speech you will ever regret.”

–Ambrose Bierce
